EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fredrock Glampground received 4 inches of rain in 1.5 hours. This resulted in Twelvemile Creek overtopping a private bridge and making portions of the campground inaccessible. Near Saco, a portion of a resident's property were inaccessible due to road washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 15th, the Quad State was positioned in the warm sector ahead of a Northern Plains low pressure system. In Southeast Missouri, effective shear was 50-70kts, MLCAPE was 1500-2000J/kg, and DCAPE was around 700-800J/kg. A capping inversion kept severe weather potential at bay but, ahead of an area of moderate rain, gusty non-thunderstorm winds blew down tree branches and some shingles in parts of Southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts measured up to 44mph at KSIK in Sikeston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 15th, the Quad State was positioned in the warm sector ahead of a Northern Plains low pressure system. In Southeast Missouri, effective shear was 50-70kts, MLCAPE was 1500-2000J/kg, and DCAPE was around 700-800J/kg. A capping inversion kept severe weather potential at bay but, ahead of an area of moderate rain, gusty non-thunderstorm winds blew down tree branches and some shingles in parts of Southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches and utility lines were downed in New Madrid from gusty non-thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 500mb shortwave trough moved across the Upper Midwest with height falls across the Quad State during the afternoon and evening of the 16th. The exit region of a strong (125kt) upper level jet was positioned across the area as well. Low-level winds strengthened during the afternoon to 50kts at 850mb. Following the early AM convection, ample sunshine allowed for a very unstable airmass to develop with CAPE of 3000-4000J/kg with deep layer shear of 65-70kts. Late afternoon, STP peaked at 9 near the Wabash/Ohio River confluence with effective SRH of 300-400m2/s2. Mid-level lapse rates peaked at 8-8.5C/km for much of the Quad State with significant hail parameter of 3-5 mid-afternoon and DCAPE peaking at 1200-1500J/kg. Initial storm formation was discrete with very large hail and tornadoes, with storms becoming more linear later in the evening with the approach of a cold front.||The afternoon supercells produced three tornadoes, one of which was a 16 mile EF-3 track from Stoddard County to Scott County which resulted in 2 fatalities and 10 injuries in Scott County, along with over a dozen homes heavily damaged or destroyed. Both fatalities occurred on Sloanville Drive west of Sandywoods, with those areas receiving the worst of the damage. An EF-1 tornado occurred east of Morley in Scott County, and an EF-2 tornado occurred east of Burfordville in Cape Girardeau County. Numerous hail reports were submitted with the supercell storms including baseball size hail in Arab and tennis ball size hail northwest of Van Buren. Towards the evening hours, straight-line wind damage occurred as the storms took on a more linear structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple quarter size hail reports were received out of Piedmont on the far northern end of a supercell storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two homes, in low-lying areas, were beginning to flood in Kennett. Sandbagging occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of hot and very humid weather began in mid July. Although thunderstorms brought some heat relief in the first few days, the heat and humidity expanded on July 22 and lasted through the 29th or 30th for many locations. While temperatures generally topped out in the mid 90s, extreme humidity led to heat index values near or over 110 degrees for a week or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot and extremely humid conditions led to heat index values over 105 degrees for a week. The heat index values peaked around 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough oriented over the Midwest, ejected a cold front towards the Mid-South. Ahead of this boundary, the Mid-South was positioned within the left entrance region of a strong midlevel jet. This feature provided increased ascent through the afternoon. Convective initiation was favored around 2-3 pm along the Tennessee/Kentucky border. Initial storm mode was supercellular, with large hail the primary concern. As this activity moved southeast, storms congealed into broken line segments, creating a greater damaging wind threat.

A 500mb shortwave trough moved across the Upper Midwest with height falls across the Quad State during the afternoon and evening of the 16th. The exit region of a strong (125kt) upper level jet was positioned across the area as well. Low-level winds strengthened during the afternoon to 50kts at 850mb. Following the early AM convection, ample sunshine allowed for a very unstable airmass to develop with CAPE of 3000-4000J/kg with deep layer shear of 65-70kts. Late afternoon, STP peaked at 9 near the Wabash/Ohio River confluence with effective SRH of 300-400m2/s2. Mid-level lapse rates peaked at 8-8.5C/km for much of the Quad State with significant hail parameter of 3-5 mid-afternoon and DCAPE peaking at 1200-1500J/kg. Initial storm formation was discrete with very large hail and tornadoes, with storms becoming more linear later in the evening with the approach of a cold front.||The afternoon supercells produced three tornadoes, one of which was a 16 mile EF-3 track from Stoddard County to Scott County which resulted in 2 fatalities and 10 injuries in Scott County, along with over a dozen homes heavily damaged or destroyed. Both fatalities occurred on Sloanville Drive west of Sandywoods, with those areas receiving the worst of the damage. An EF-1 tornado occurred east of Morley in Scott County, and an EF-2 tornado occurred east of Burfordville in Cape Girardeau County. Numerous hail reports were submitted with the supercell storms including baseball size hail in Arab and tennis ball size hail northwest of Van Buren. Towards the evening hours, straight-line wind damage occurred as the storms took on a more linear structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was measured in Kelso on the north end of an eastward propagating supercell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of hot and very humid weather began in mid July. Although thunderstorms brought some heat relief in the first few days, the heat and humidity expanded on July 22 and lasted through the 29th or 30th for many locations. While temperatures generally topped out in the mid 90s, extreme humidity led to heat index values near or over 110 degrees for a week or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot and extremely humid conditions led to heat index values over 105 degrees for several days. The heat index values peaked around 112 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 15th, the Quad State was positioned in the warm sector ahead of a Northern Plains low pressure system. In Southeast Missouri, effective shear was 50-70kts, MLCAPE was 1500-2000J/kg, and DCAPE was around 700-800J/kg. A capping inversion kept severe weather potential at bay but, ahead of an area of moderate rain, gusty non-thunderstorm winds blew down tree branches and some shingles in parts of Southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of tree limbs down and some shingles blown off a building was reported south of Neelyville with winds estimated at 50mph. The Poplar Bluff ASOS reported wind gusts to 44mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant multi-hazard, multi-day event occurred across the Mid-South from April 2, 2025, to April 8, 2025, producing 35 tornadoes, record flooding, and numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.  A large upper-level trough covered the Western U.S. in early April. A significant piece of energy rotated around the base of the trough and ejected into the Southern Plains and the Middle-Upper Mississippi Valley on April 2nd. A 500 mb jet maximum of 120 knots and a 300 mb jet maximum of 140 knots pushed into Iowa by late afternoon on April 2nd.  Meanwhile, a 992 mb surface low moved into the Upper Mississippi Valley with a trailing cold front pushing toward the Mid-South. A secondary, weaker surface low developed over northeast Arkansas and helped to back surface winds. The warm sector across the Mid-South was potent with surface dewpoints climbing into the upper 60s and MLCAPE values climbing to 2000-3000 J/kg. Increasing upper-level divergence occurred in the entrance region of the upper jet, resulting in strong lift across the Mid-South and storm initiation in the increasingly moist and unstable airmass. Hodographs were long, strongly curved, and supportive of tornadoes. Discrete supercells intensified across the Mid-South during the evening as 0-6 km bulk shear values increased to 60-70 knots, and 0-1 km helicity values increased to 300-400 m2/s2. Fifteen tornadoes occurred from late afternoon on April 2nd through about 2 am on April 3rd, including four EF-3s. This period represented the most significant period of severe weather during the event. ||The cold front stalled across the Mid-South on April 3rd while moist southwesterly flow aloft continued and anomalous precipitable water values prevailed across the region. Heavy rain fell across northern sections of the Mid-South into the morning hours of April 3rd. Rainfall amounts of 3-4 inches were common across east-central Arkansas, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi, along with scattered instances of flash flooding. By the afternoon of April 3rd, the airmass south of the stalled front destabilized and storms strengthened thanks to a 90-knot mid-level jet maximum moving through Missouri. These storms produced wind damage, large hail, flash flooding, and a tornado east of Corinth. Heavy rain continued into the early morning hours on April 4th when the front eventually lifted north of the area, resulting in a relative lull during the day on April 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and large limbs down around southwest portions of the county. Minor roof damage to a few structures was also noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat dome continued to strengthen and expand across the eastern CONUS during late June. The upper level pattern was dominated by a large 594 dam Bermuda High over the eastern US with a deep trough over the West Coast. This pattern persisted for several days before the ridge finally broke down and the westerlies advanced further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index value of 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front eventually sagged south back into the Mid-South during the early morning hours of April 5th. Strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in heavy rain and training storms across northern sections of the Mid-South, mainly north of I-40. A bow echo developed along and just south of the slowly advancing front. This feature crossed the entire forecast area producing 13 tornadoes, including an EF-2, and wind damage. Heavy rain continued through the morning hours with flash flooding increasing across the Mid-South. ||The final round of severe weather occurred during the afternoon and evening of April 5th. The slow-moving cold front combined with an increasingly unstable airmass, strong upper-level divergence, and 0-6 km bulk shear values of 50-60 knots provided the ingredients for more severe storms. A QLCS with a bookend vortex pushed into east Arkansas and West TN with a good deal of wind damage. The southern end of the line became more supercellular during the afternoon and evening and produced seven tornadoes, including an EF-2. The combination of continued abundant moisture with precipitable water values greater than 1.75 inches and strong low-level moisture transport into the frontal zone resulted in very heavy rainfall and considerable flash flooding across much of the Mid-South. ||The severe weather threat finally wound down by late evening on April 5th with the flash flood threat ending a few hours later. Total rainfall amounts of 10-15 inches occurred across parts of east Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel, West Tennessee, and extreme northwest Mississippi during this event. This resulted in widespread river flooding for several days following the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few homes were flooded in Kennett, along Hwy 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds under a building upper-level ridge pushed air temperatures into the mid 90s along with oppressive humidity. Heat indices peaked in the range of 105 to 109 degrees during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS reported a maximum heat index of 106 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of June 3rd, a line of thunderstorms developed in northeast Missouri, and spread into far southeast Iowa and Illinois.  These storms rapidly became severe. The thunderstorms quickly moved northeast across southeast Iowa, west central, and northwest Illinois between 530 PM and 8 PM CDT.  This line of storms produced 8 tornadoes, along with isolated pockets of higher end wind damage from estimated winds of 80 to 100 mph. A cluster of several tornadoes moved into the the southern portions of the Quad Cities IA/IL metro area, producing EF1 damage into Moline Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A summer-like pattern was in place across the Mid-South. The severe weather parameter space lacked adequate shear there was decent instability available. A few storms were able to tap into the available fuel and produce pulse-like hailers with a damaging wind gust or two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed powerlines at the corner of 6th St and Eastwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge originated over the Carolinas and gradually retrograded over the Mid-South with heat building across the Mid-South. Eventually, the ridge was centered over Mississippi, and extreme heat and humidity prevailed to close out July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a moist airmass in place over the region, afternoon pulse thunderstorms developed across southwestern Missouri producing localized wind damage and flooding. Significant flooding occurred in eastern Greene County where between 3 and 6 inches of rain fall over a less than 6 hour period as persistent slow moving storms developed over the county. During the period, some locations saw in excess of an inch of rain in a 15 minute period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measuring 60 mph wind were recorded on a home weather station.  Hail up to penny size was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Oppressive heat continued to plague the Mid-South as an intense ridge retrograded across the Lower Mississippi Valley. Highs ranged from the upper 90s to the lower 100s. Dewpoints across the region soared into the upper 70s and low 80s. This combination led to dangerous heat indices greater than 110 degrees nearly areawide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 116 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms initiated along and ahead of a quasi-stationary boundary during the afternoon of July 7th. The environment was characterized by high moisture and instability and weak shear, leading to gusty winds being the primary threat for the day. Thunderstorms overperformed, producing isolated wind damage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed multiple tree limbs in the area. Billboards were blown away and an RV was tipped over at the I-70 RV Center. Multiple RVs were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a moist airmass in place over the region, afternoon pulse thunderstorms developed across southwestern Missouri producing localized wind damage and flooding. During the period, some locations saw in excess of an inch of rain in a 15 minute period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large 2-3 inch diameter branches were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening and early morning hours of July 20th/21st, an MCS moved southeast through east-central Missouri. The front edge of the system produced very efficient and heavy rainfall, causing flash flood conditions quickly. This was exacerbated in portions of eastern Audrain County which received multiple rounds of this heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused numerous roads to become impassable in Audrain County. These include Highway 54 in Farber, two unnamed roads north of Laddonia, many roads in Laddonia proper, Highway BB just west of Highway W near Middletown, and Highway J at Highway B north of Rush Hill. A water rescue was completed on Highway 54 between Farber and Bassinger Corner. Another vehicle was stuck in water in front of the POET Biorefinery. A third vehicle was stuck in water on Highway 19 in Martinsburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small boat dock was flipped over and multiple trees were snapped by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of hot and very humid weather began in mid July. Although thunderstorms brought some heat relief in the first few days, the heat and humidity expanded on July 22 and lasted through the 29th or 30th for many locations. While temperatures generally topped out in the mid 90s, extreme humidity led to heat index values near or over 110 degrees for a week or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot and extremely humid conditions led to heat index values over 105 degrees for several days. The heat index values peaked around 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night of July 26th-July 27th, moisture convergence along a low-level jet spurred convection in eastern Missouri. This convection developed in an environment primed for efficient and heavy rainfall. Multiple rounds of thunderstorms trained over Lincoln County, producing flash flooding mainly in Elsberry where a state of emergency was declared. In all, there were 42 water rescues and 70 to 80 homes and businesses were affected. Cost estimates were upward of $5 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in 42 water rescues in Lincoln County. The only official report of a road closure was MO-79 at Brownsmill Road where swift moving floodwater was reported. In Elsberry, 50 homes and 20 to 30 businesses were affected by the flooding, and a state of emergency was declared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, stationary heat dome brought oppressive heat to the Mid-South in mid to late August. The region was fed by warm, moist air from the Gulf, which elevated dewpoints and thus made heat-index values much higher than the actual air temperature alone. Because cloud cover was suppressed and the ground surface was dry, nighttime cooling was limited, elevating heat stress. The heat dome remained parked for several days, resulting in minimal forecast change or relief. Finally, an upper-level disturbance was able to bring relief as showers and thunderstorm chances returned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 108 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged through the Quad State from north to south later in the day on the 19th. A jagged line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across Southeast Missouri and Southern Illinois, dipping southward through late afternoon. MLCAPE maxed out early afternoon around 3000-3500J/kg with DCAPE of 1200J/kg. Effective shear was limited, below 30kts. Wind damage, mainly to trees, was most widespread in Cape Girardeau County as a storm tracked southeastward through the county during the early afternoon. Wind damage was also reported in Butler County with hail noted in Carter County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along with quarter size hail (measured), the storm also produced wind gusts estimated at 50-55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad high pressure over the middle Mississippi River Valley resulted in temperatures surging to the mid-to-upper 90s with dew points in the mid 70s, yielding heat indices around 105-112 mainly from the 15th-19th in Southeast Missouri with some locally higher values. A few sites reached heat indices high enough for duration based heat criteria starting on the 14th, mainly in the Ozark Foothills. A cold front the night of the 19th-20th brought an end to the heat. The highest observed heat index was 118 on the 18th at the Big Springs RAWS site.

A shortwave trough and closed low reached the Quad State from the northwest during the morning of the 4th. The arrival of the shortwave preceded peak heating by several hours, limiting convective potential. Shear was very weak and instability limited. Slow-moving storms produced heavy rain, and multiple trees blown down in Delta by straight-line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Delta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Erin dominated the upper-level pattern and steered the remnants of an MCS directly towards the Mid-South. Shower and thunderstorm coverage was much higher than in previous days as storms tapped into moderate to high instability. Storms were able to quickly grow upscale and produce damaging winds and some sub-severe hail. Several outflow boundaries were produced from convective coverage with measured 40-50 mph winds associated with the boundaries. These winds were enough to produce sporadic damage in addition to stronger winds from severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down on East Commercial St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A longwave trough slowly crossing the region was able to pull sufficient Gulf moisture into Missouri. This moisture combined with 90 degree air temperatures was enough for heat index values to exceed 105 degrees. Afternoon shower and thunderstorm coverage was minimal and only provided temporary relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum heat index of 107 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By mid October, D2 (severe drought) conditions had developed across several counties in western Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation deficits over the past few months created drought (D2) conditions in much of Jackson County by the Oct 14, 2025 issuance of the US Drought Monitor. This would continue through the end of October 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By mid October, D2 (severe drought) conditions had developed across several counties in western Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation deficits over the past few months created drought (D2) conditions in much of Cass County by the Oct 14, 2025 issuance of the US Drought Monitor. This would continue through the end of October 2025.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought developed across portions of the Missouri Ozarks. Locations in central and southern Missouri saw drought conditions worsen through the month after  continued limited rainfall during the month of October. Extreme (D3) Drought developed across portions of southwestern Missouri. Much of the remainder of far southeastern Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks was also in Severe (D2) and Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions by the end of the month. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought improved, however the majority of losses will be added once  crop figures are determined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed across all of the county during the month. Conditions worsened through the month after limited rainfall during the month. Significant impacts were noted in surface water including ponds and streams with some produces reporting having to feed hay and culling herds. Some impacts to crop yield were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 2025 saw marked improvement in drought conditions across the Missouri Bootheel, driven mainly by above-average rainfall. While severe drought (D2) and moderate drought (D1) continued in parts of the region at times during the month, the overall trend was toward diminished drought intensity and location by late October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) Conditions improved to Moderate Drought (D1) conditions early in October across Dunklin County.

The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought improved, however the majority of losses will be added once  crop figures are determined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought developed across western and southeastern portions of the county during the month. Conditions worsened through the month after limited rainfall during the month. The remainder of the county was impacted by Severe (D2) to Moderate (D1) drought conditions at the end of the month. Significant impacts were noted in surface water including ponds and streams with some produces reporting having to feed hay and culling herds. Some impacts to crop yield were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions from September continued into October, with portions of Southeast Missouri from Van Buren to Charleston in the south, and Perry County to the north, entering October in D2 (Severe Drought). Drought onset began with a very dry August and first half of September, with a rainy late September. Some early month rainfall improved drought status near the Bootheel. Later on, heavy rain on October 18th in the north led to improved drought conditions for Perry County. Carter and Ripley Counties were the only ones in the entire Quad State region to have D2 drought linger into November. Harvest season was ongoing, resulting in limited additional agricultural impacts for Southeast Missouri.

A storm system of mainly multi-cluster strong to severe storms moved across the Ozarks producing localized reports of quarter sized hail. Additionally, strong winds downed several trees and caused power outages with limbs and trees on powerlines. At least one structure was struck by lightning causing damage by fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two barns and a home were reported to have been struck by lightning. Small fires occurred with the barns and the front porch of the home partially collapsed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Joplin airport measured a 64 mph non thunderstorm wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was measured at the Springfield Branson National Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month began with Severe (D2) drought lingering across the Ozarks. Little to no improvement and some expansion of drought conditions occurred through the month. By the end of the month, of Severe (D2) Drought conditions remained with Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions remaining as well. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought lingered through the end of the year. Losses were determined through the end of 2025 with figures primarily focused on agricultural losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite well below the monthly average rainfall little changed with drought conditions through the month leaving Severe (D2) drought conditions in the county by the end of the month. Impacts across the region were largely limited to larger scale agriculture. However, farm and stock ponds saw impacts with ground water levels also falling. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought lingered through the end of the year. Losses were determined through the end of 2025 with figures primarily focused on agricultural losses.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a result of well below the monthly average rainfall drought conditions expanded through the month leaving Severe (D2) drought conditions in the southwestern portions  of the county by the end of the month. Impacts across the region were largely limited to larger scale agriculture. However, farm and stock ponds saw impacts with ground water levels also falling. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought lingered through the end of the year. Losses were determined through the end of 2025 with figures primarily focused on agricultural losses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the mid morning hours on Monday, December 1, just 2 days after a much heavier snow fell on November 29. Although total snowfall amount were only a couple of inches, snowfall rates were briefly heavy and the temperature was very cold leading to a quick accumulation on roadways. The snow arrived earlier than expected leading many people caught off guard by its arrival. A rapid increase in traffic volume occurred just as roadways had become snow covered and slippery. Numerous accidents occurred leading to many road closures. Some were reportedly stuck in traffic for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow covered roads led to multiple accidents. Although only 2 to 3 inches of snow was reported, accidents caused several injuries and road closures led to long traffic delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter storm tracked across the region Friday night through Saturday.  There were widespread snow reports of 6 to 10 inches across northeast Missouri. In addition to the snow, strong winds developed behind this storm system as cold air flooded into the region with gusts of 30 to 40 MPH across northeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer near Memphis reported 9.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S. with the Quad State initially on the edge of the ridge from the 16th-18th, resulting in greater coverage of showers and thunderstorms. Heat issues continued through the end of July with no interruption for the southernmost two rows of counties.||High temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to near 80) produced heat index values in the 100 to 112 range from July 16th through the 18th for most of Southeast Missouri, with isolated higher values. Some relief came for northern portions of the region on the 18th due to widespread showers/storms and cloud cover. ||The highest heat index in Southeast Missouri for the 16th-18th was 120 at the Big Spring RAWS site in Carter County on the 16th.

An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. The Quad State was initially on the edge of the ridge from the 17th-20th, resulting in greater coverage of showers and thunderstorms. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th-29th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||For Southeast Missouri, high temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s) produced heat index values routinely in the 102 to 112 range from July 19th through the 30th. Some relief came during the northern portions of Southeast Missouri on the 21st due to showers and increased cloud cover. As the ridge amplified and shower activity decreased, Southeast Missouri became covered in widespread heat indices of 105-115 from the 22nd-25th, with localized higher readings. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 26th-27th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-120 with localized higher readings on the 28th-29th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. Heat Advisories or Extreme Heat Warnings were in effect for portions of the Quad State for 15 consecutive days.||Sikeston, MO reached a heat index of 110+ for 10 consecutive days and exceeded 105 degrees every day during the 15 day heat wave. Cape Girardeau, MO broke their all-time record for consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point with 26 straight days from July 6-31, while 49 consecutive days reaching a 70 degree dew point was one shy of the record. Cape Girardeau observed 10 straight days of a heat index of at least 105 from July 16th-25th, tying their longest streak in 1999, while reaching 100 degrees 16 straight days was one shy of their record (heat index records date back to 1996 for Cape Girardeau). Poplar Bluff, MO experienced 25 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point from July 6-30, which is tied for the 3rd longest streak on record, while 51 consecutive days with a dew point of 70 was one shy of the record. Poplar Bluff observed 9 consecutive days reaching a heat index of at least 105 degrees from July 17th-26th, which was three days shy of the all-time longest streak, while their 15-day streak of reaching 100 degrees is tied for second longest (heat index records date back to 1997 for Poplar Bluff). The highest heat index in Southeast Missouri for the late July heatwave was 122 at the Big Spring RAWS site in Carter County on the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low drifted southeastward from Missouri through Southern Illinois and Western Kentucky during the evening hours of the 18th. Areas to the south with some afternoon clearing allowed for instability to develop, resulting in the formation of multiple clusters of storms that tracked southeastward. The most prominent of these clusters strengthened in far Southeast Missouri, moving across the Mississippi River into far Western Kentucky, at times developing embedded supercell structure. This cluster produced hail in Scott City but peaked near and just across the Mississippi River, producing a 61mph wind gust at Dorena. Effective bulk shear peaked at around 50kts near the Missouri/Kentucky border with DCAPE of 800J/kg to go along with the MLCAPE of over 1000J/kg. Precipitable water peaked at 1.3in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media received several reports of quarter size hail measured in Scott City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month began with Severe (D2) drought lingering across the Ozarks. Locations across west central and southern Missouri saw drought conditions improve through the  month thanks to some beneficial rainfall. By the end of the month, only pockets of Severe (D2) Drought conditions remained with Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions remaining as well. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought improved, however the majority of losses will be added once crop figures are determined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though below the monthly average, timely rain fell across the region through the month. This allowed for drought improvements to Moderate (D1) drought by the end of the month. Impacts across the region were largely limited to larger scale agriculture. However, farm and stock ponds saw impacts with ground water levels also falling. Crop loss estimates notated are for fall harvest loses from the summer into the fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. The Quad State was initially on the edge of the ridge from the 17th-20th, resulting in greater coverage of showers and thunderstorms. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th-29th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||For Southeast Missouri, high temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s) produced heat index values routinely in the 102 to 112 range from July 19th through the 30th. Some relief came during the northern portions of Southeast Missouri on the 21st due to showers and increased cloud cover. As the ridge amplified and shower activity decreased, Southeast Missouri became covered in widespread heat indices of 105-115 from the 22nd-25th, with localized higher readings. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 26th-27th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-120 with localized higher readings on the 28th-29th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. Heat Advisories or Extreme Heat Warnings were in effect for portions of the Quad State for 15 consecutive days.||Sikeston, MO reached a heat index of 110+ for 10 consecutive days and exceeded 105 degrees every day during the 15 day heat wave. Cape Girardeau, MO broke their all-time record for consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point with 26 straight days from July 6-31, while 49 consecutive days reaching a 70 degree dew point was one shy of the record. Cape Girardeau observed 10 straight days of a heat index of at least 105 from July 16th-25th, tying their longest streak in 1999, while reaching 100 degrees 16 straight days was one shy of their record (heat index records date back to 1996 for Cape Girardeau). Poplar Bluff, MO experienced 25 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point from July 6-30, which is tied for the 3rd longest streak on record, while 51 consecutive days with a dew point of 70 was one shy of the record. Poplar Bluff observed 9 consecutive days reaching a heat index of at least 105 degrees from July 17th-26th, which was three days shy of the all-time longest streak, while their 15-day streak of reaching 100 degrees is tied for second longest (heat index records date back to 1997 for Poplar Bluff). The highest heat index in Southeast Missouri for the late July heatwave was 122 at the Big Spring RAWS site in Carter County on the 22nd.

A surface boundary was positioned west to east across the Quad State during the early afternoon with convective activity increasing with daytime heating. Scattered convection primarily occurred south of the boundary mid to late afternoon, at which point the better storm potential was east of the Mississippi. The one report out of Southeast Missouri was a midday sighting of a funnel cloud east of Bernie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of a funnel cloud east of Bernie.

A very slow moving warm front stretched from west to east across southern portions of the Quad State the evening of the 20th into the early morning hours of the 21st. Pockets of intermittent dense fog developed, at times accompanied by light rain, and generally with calm or 3mph winds. A powerful outflow boundary from the cell toppled a center pivot irrigation system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pivot irrigation system was tipped over by a thunderstorm outflow. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 27th, the Quad State was in the middle of an extended heat wave, with high dew points contributing to a very moist environment. As a band of afternoon thunderstorms took shape in southern portions of the Ozarks, drifting southward into Arkansas, torrential rainfall rates produced flooding in Naylor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall filled ditches in Naylor, with ponding on roadways, in a park, and around grain bins. Radar estimated rainfall rates peaked over 2'' an hour in Naylor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A morning frontal boundary on the northern edge of the Quad State lifted a little northward during the day on the 15th. A 40-50kt jet out of the WSW continued into the Quad State, bringing enough Gulf moisture to keep PWATs over 2 inches. MLCAPE values reached 2000-2500J/kg, while effective bulk shear remained limited. In Southeast Missouri, scattered showers and thunderstorms occurred through the day. A funnel cloud was observed with a storm over Neelyville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo was shared of a tropical funnel occurring with a thunderstorm over Neelyville.

A slow moving cold frontal boundary entered into the Quad State on the 12th. Showers and thunderstorms formed on the south side of the front and moved eastward through the region during the afternoon and early evening hours. Afternoon storms produced tree damage on the north side of Cape Girardeau. MLCAPE was around 2000J/kg, DCAPE was 900J/kg, and effective bulk shear was 20kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large 6in diameter tree branch was downed by downburst winds on the north side of Cape Girardeau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Missouri was in the midst of a prolonged heat wave and the extreme heat and humidity supported a cluster of thunderstorms in the late afternoon and early evening. The storms moved southwestward across northwest Wayne County and through Carter County.  Occasional downbursts resulted in sporadic damaging winds from the Clearwater Lake area to Van Buren.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down three trees near the intersection of Highway D and Jason Avenue just north of Van Buren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month began with Severe (D2) drought lingering across the Ozarks. Locations across west central and southern Missouri saw drought conditions improve through the  month thanks to some beneficial rainfall. By the end of the month, only pockets of Severe (D2) Drought conditions remained with Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions remaining as well. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought improved, however the majority of losses will be added once crop figures are determined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though below the monthly average, timely rain fell across the region through the month. Despite the rains, little to no drought improvements occurred the the Severe (D2) drought conditions in the county by the end of the month. Impacts across the region were largely limited to larger scale agriculture. However, farm and stock ponds saw impacts with ground water levels also falling. Crop loss estimates will be noted when drought conditions improve beyond Severe (D2) drought conditions or by the end of the year.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions developed in the Ozark Foothills of Southeast Missouri early September due to a very dry August and first half of September. Conditions improved with October rainfall over most of Southeast Missouri but northwestern Carter County and southwestern Ripley County retained D2 (Severe Drought) status into November. November rainfall in those areas was around an inch below normal, allowing D2 drought to continue into December over roughly the same area. With the growing season having ended, impacts were very limited in November.

Locations across west central and southern Missouri saw drought conditions improve through the  month thanks to some beneficial rainfall. By the end of the month, only pockets of Severe (D2) Drought conditions remained with Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions remaining as well. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought improved, however the majority of losses will be added once crop figures are determined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though below the monthly average, timely rain fell across the region through the month. Despite the rains, little to no drought improvements occurred the the Severe (D2) drought conditions in the county by the end of the month. Impacts across the region were largely limited to larger scale agriculture. However, farm and stock ponds saw impacts with ground water levels also falling. Crop loss estimates will be noted when drought conditions improve beyond Severe (D2) drought conditions or by the end of the year.

Snow began in the early morning hours of Saturday, November 29 and quickly became heavy. Snowfall rates reached 1 inch per hour or more for a time. Snow gradually transitioned to rain as the temperature warmed up after sunrise, but several inches had already blanketed the region by then. This was a heavy, wet snowfall that quickly coated roads and led to hazardous travel conditions primarily Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 7 to 9 inches of snow fell primarily during the morning hours. The snowfall rate reached 1 inch per hour at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. The Quad State was initially on the edge of the ridge from the 17th-20th, resulting in greater coverage of showers and thunderstorms. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th-29th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||For Southeast Missouri, high temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s) produced heat index values routinely in the 102 to 112 range from July 19th through the 30th. Some relief came during the northern portions of Southeast Missouri on the 21st due to showers and increased cloud cover. As the ridge amplified and shower activity decreased, Southeast Missouri became covered in widespread heat indices of 105-115 from the 22nd-25th, with localized higher readings. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 26th-27th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-120 with localized higher readings on the 28th-29th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. Heat Advisories or Extreme Heat Warnings were in effect for portions of the Quad State for 15 consecutive days.||Sikeston, MO reached a heat index of 110+ for 10 consecutive days and exceeded 105 degrees every day during the 15 day heat wave. Cape Girardeau, MO broke their all-time record for consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point with 26 straight days from July 6-31, while 49 consecutive days reaching a 70 degree dew point was one shy of the record. Cape Girardeau observed 10 straight days of a heat index of at least 105 from July 16th-25th, tying their longest streak in 1999, while reaching 100 degrees 16 straight days was one shy of their record (heat index records date back to 1996 for Cape Girardeau). Poplar Bluff, MO experienced 25 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point from July 6-30, which is tied for the 3rd longest streak on record, while 51 consecutive days with a dew point of 70 was one shy of the record. Poplar Bluff observed 9 consecutive days reaching a heat index of at least 105 degrees from July 17th-26th, which was three days shy of the all-time longest streak, while their 15-day streak of reaching 100 degrees is tied for second longest (heat index records date back to 1997 for Poplar Bluff). The highest heat index in Southeast Missouri for the late July heatwave was 122 at the Big Spring RAWS site in Carter County on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat continued from mid-July. Heat indices only reached heat advisory duration criteria July 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms formed for the late morning hours in Southeast Missouri. A cold air funnel was observed east of Bernie. MLCAPE values were over 1000J/kg while wind shear was weak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold air funnel was visible east of Bernie, roughly two-thirds of the way down to the surface. A public photo of it was relayed by emergency management.

As the ridge amplified and shower activity decreased, Southeast Missouri became covered in widespread heat indices of 105-115 from the 22nd-25th, with localized higher readings. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 26th-27th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-120 with localized higher readings on the 28th-29th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. Heat Advisories or Extreme Heat Warnings were in effect for portions of the Quad State for 15 consecutive days.||Sikeston, MO reached a heat index of 110+ for 10 consecutive days and exceeded 105 degrees every day during the 15 day heat wave. Cape Girardeau, MO broke their all-time record for consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point with 26 straight days from July 6-31, while 49 consecutive days reaching a 70 degree dew point was one shy of the record. Cape Girardeau observed 10 straight days of a heat index of at least 105 from July 16th-25th, tying their longest streak in 1999, while reaching 100 degrees 16 straight days was one shy of their record (heat index records date back to 1996 for Cape Girardeau). Poplar Bluff, MO experienced 25 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point from July 6-30, which is tied for the 3rd longest streak on record, while 51 consecutive days with a dew point of 70 was one shy of the record. Poplar Bluff observed 9 consecutive days reaching a heat index of at least 105 degrees from July 17th-26th, which was three days shy of the all-time longest streak, while their 15-day streak of reaching 100 degrees is tied for second longest (heat index records date back to 1997 for Poplar Bluff). The highest heat index in Southeast Missouri for the late July heatwave was 122 at the Big Spring RAWS site in Carter County on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme heat continued from mid-July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. The Quad State was initially on the edge of the ridge from the 17th-20th, resulting in greater coverage of showers and thunderstorms. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th-29th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||For Southeast Missouri, high temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to low 80s) produced heat index values routinely in the 102 to 112 range from July 19th through the 30th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month began with Severe (D2) drought lingering across the Ozarks. Locations across west central and southern Missouri saw drought conditions improve through the  month thanks to some beneficial rainfall. By the end of the month, only pockets of Severe (D2) Drought conditions remained with Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions remaining as well. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Some limited monetary losses were noted in areas where the drought improved, however the majority of losses will be added once crop figures are determined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though below the monthly average, timely rain fell across the region through the month. This allowed for drought improvements to Moderate (D1) drought by the end of the month. Impacts across the region were largely limited to larger scale agriculture. However, farm and stock ponds saw impacts with ground water levels also falling. Crop loss estimates notated are for fall harvest loses from the summer into the fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface low pressure system moved into Missouri, a warm front lifted into northern portions of the Quad State region. Ahead of the front, a SW to NE band of showers and thunderstorms developed, pushing eastward through mid-morning. Penny size hail was reported on the north side of Cape Girardeau with these storms. A lack of instability prevented greater storm potential despite the effective bulk shear of 30-40kts and precipitable water values of 1.1in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was measured as a storm tracked eastward through Cape Girardeau county, with the report submitted via mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in September, soil moisture was well below normal, and river flows were below typical values due to lack of beneficial rainfall. Severe (D2) Drought conditions expanded and worsened to Extreme (D3) Drought conditions for a brief period. By late September, conditions improved somewhat in terms of soil moisture and crop indices, yet underlying water-resource concerns (streams/rivers) and elevated fire risk remained. Beneficial rainfall resulted in the improvement of drought conditions by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought conditions were present at the beginning of September before worsening to Extreme (D3) conditions for a brief period mid-month. Slight improvement occurred by the end of the month as D3 conditions ended, but D2 conditions prevailed and continued into early October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed across portions of the Missouri Ozarks. Locations in central and southern Missouri saw drought conditions worsen through the month after a record dry August and continued limited rainfall during the month of September. Severe (D2) Drought developed first across southern Missouri and expanded somewhat into portions of central Missouri by months end. Much of the remainder of far southeastern Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks was also in Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions by the end of the month. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Monetary losses will likely not be readily assessable until crop figures are determined but any that occur will be noted at the end of the year or when drought conditions improve from Severe (D2) Drought levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed across northern portions of the county during the month. Conditions worsened through the month after a record dry August and continued limited rainfall during the month of September. The remainder of the county was impacted by Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) drought conditions at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and moist conditions paired with weak surface convergence resulted in diurnally driven isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon of September 14th. One of these thunderstorms uprooted a tree in Jefferson City, MO.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos on Facebook show a healthy tree uprooted by thunderstorm winds in a neighborhood near the Cole County Fairgrounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in Missouri along a backdoor cold front during the afternoon of September 15th. An unstable but weakly sheared environment with steep low-level lapse rates resulted in the potential for microbursts from these thunderstorms. A few microbursts did occur, with wind damage ranging from downed trees to a partially removed school roof.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed multiple trees just east of Millersburg.

A member of the public estimated 60 mph winds with the thunderstorm.

A front approached southern Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours of September 23. Several thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. Some storms produced damaging winds in Dallas and Webster counties. Several instances of flash flooding occurred across McDonald, Barry and Taney counties where 2-3 inches of rainfall occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped tree branches and caused minor damage to siding panels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed across portions of the Missouri Ozarks. Locations in central and southern Missouri saw drought conditions worsen through the month after a record dry August and continued limited rainfall during the month of September. Severe (D2) Drought developed first across southern Missouri and expanded somewhat into portions of central Missouri by months end. Much of the remainder of far southeastern Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks was also in Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions by the end of the month. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Monetary losses will likely not be readily assessable until crop figures are determined but any that occur will be noted at the end of the year or when drought conditions improve from Severe (D2) Drought levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed across western portions of the county during the month. Conditions worsened through the month after a record dry August and continued limited rainfall during the month of September. The remainder of the county was impacted by Moderate (D1) to Abnormally Dry (D0) drought conditions at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enhanced moisture convergence near a surface low allowed a few storms to develop over the Ozark Foothills of southeast Missouri in the afternoon. With high pressure aloft centered north of the area, wind shear was very weak, but surface-based CAPE was around 2000 J/kg. As they moved southward in the late afternoon, one storm produced some hail near the Arkansas border south of Gatewood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell to the south of Gatewood near the Fourche Creek Conservation Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought impacted portions of the Missouri Ozarks briefly through the middle of September across Hickory, Camden and Miller Counties after a record dry August and continued limited rainfall during the month of September. Severe (D2) Drought developed first across southern Missouri and expanded somewhat into portions of central Missouri by months end. By the 23rd of the month some rainfall allowed conditions to improve to Moderate (D1) Drought. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Monetary losses will likely not be readily assessable until crop figures are determined but any that occur will be noted at the end of the year or if impacts are reported after the brief period of severe drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) Drought developed first across southeastern portions of the county on the 16th and expanded somewhat into portions of central Missouri by through the 23rd of the month when some rainfall allowed conditions to improve to Moderate (D1) Drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very dry August, during which most of Southeast Missouri receiving an inch of rain or less, including the 2nd driest August on record at Poplar Bluff and 6th driest August at Cape Girardeau, at least Severe Drought (D2) conditions developed in all of Southeast Missouri during September. Most of Southeast Missouri, from Van Buren and Doniphan to Sikeston and New Madrid, entered Severe Drought early September. Mid-month, Severe Drought expanded across the remainder of Southeast Missouri, and an area from Butler to Mississippi Counties rose to Extreme Drought (D3). Heavy rainfall from the 20th-24th resulted in significant improvement in drought conditions with Severe Drought only continuing into October for the Van Buren to Charleston corridor, despite above normal September rainfall in that area, and Perry County, which received below normal September rainfall. Agricultural impacts from the drought occurred heading into harvest season. Burn bans were briefly instituted in some areas as a result of the drought. Several field fires occurred in Southeast Missouri during September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary stretching across portions of Southeast Missouri resulted in thunderstorms with heavy rain during the early morning hours of the 23rd from Carter County southeastward to New Madrid. A broader area of rainfall arrived late morning which produced additional heavy rains. Flooding issues developed in Southeastern Carter County which received some of the higher totals from both rounds. Precipitable water values were around 1.8in. 850mb moisture transport was not particularly strong, but was pointed towards the NW to SE oriented early morning heavy rainfall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding issues developed in Southeastern Carter County between Ellsinore and Grandin as early and late morning rounds of heavy rainfall combined to result in a radar estimated 2.5-5 inches of rainfall over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed across portions of the Missouri Ozarks. Locations in central and southern Missouri saw drought conditions worsen through the month after a record dry August and continued limited rainfall during the month of September. Severe (D2) Drought developed first across southern Missouri and expanded somewhat into portions of central Missouri by months end. Much of the remainder of far southeastern Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks was also in Moderate (D1) Drought and Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions by the end of the month. The primary impacts of the drought occurred in the agricultural communities. Dry stock ponds and impacts to crop yields and end of the year forage production. Low flows on some streams and rivers were noted but not widespread. Monetary losses will likely not be readily assessable until crop figures are determined but any that occur will be noted at the end of the year or when drought conditions improve from Severe (D2) Drought levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed across the southern three fourths of the county during the month. Conditions worsened through the month after a record dry August and continued limited rainfall during the month of September. The remainder of the county was impacted by Moderate (D1) drought conditions at the end of the month.
